notable.notable_blurb: San Francisco police judge then county judge

education.notes: Several terms at Grand River Institute Austinburg OH.
Finished his education at Western Reserve College.

career.title: Cleveland lawyer
career.notes: Admitted to the Ohio bar 1847. Partnered first with Loren Prentiss; in 1850 parted with Prentiss to join a new enterprise with childhood friend Edwin P. Mastick. Cleveland practice focused on criminal defense (clients accused of rape, arson, insanity), real estate transactions, and a range of civil matters.

career.title: Secretary-Treasurer, Cataract Fire Company No. V
career.organization: Cataract Fire Company No. V, Cleveland
career.notes: Volunteer fire-company role during his Cleveland years.

career.title: California migration
career.notes: Migrated to San Francisco with childhood friend and law partner Edwin P. Mastick — both 'struck with California fever' two years after the diary ends.

career.title: Deputy Clerk, Supreme Court of California
career.notes: First California position upon arriving in San Francisco.

career.title: Member, San Francisco Vigilance Committee
career.organization: San Francisco Committee of Vigilance of 1856
career.notes: Active member of the 1856 Vigilance Committee — one of the largest extralegal civic-purification movements in 19th-century US history.

career.title: San Francisco Police Judge
career.notes: Elected Police Judge — also one of three commissioners selecting the SF police force during a period of civic reform. (Earlier project notes had this as 1856; correct dates per the new Carpe Librum biographical source are 1860-1863.)

career.title: County Judge of San Francisco
career.notes: Presided over the SF County Court for four years; recorded decisions form a significant chapter in California jurisprudence.

career.title: Member, Committee of Safety
career.notes: Active during the 1877 SF railroad strikes.

career.title: President, Savings and Loan Society (Clay Street Bank)
career.organization: Savings and Loan Society, San Francisco
career.notes: Long-time legal adviser to the Clay Street Bank; elected its president 1879. Held the post until his Nov 17, 1880 death.

career.title: Senior partner, Cowles & Brown
career.notes: Returned to private SF practice 1868; partnership with Albert N. Brown until his 1880 death.

narrative_blocks.header: San Francisco police then county judge 1860-1868
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel was elected San Francisco Police Judge in 1860 — also one of three commissioners selecting the SF police force — and served the County Court bench from 1863 to 1868. His recorded decisions form a significant chapter in California jurisprudence during the state's formative years.

narrative_blocks.header: Member of San Francisco's 1856 Vigilance Committee
narrative_blocks.body: An earnest and efficient worker in 'the great social purification' of San Francisco, Samuel served the 1856 Vigilance Committee — one of the largest extralegal civic movements in 19th-century US history. He gloried in those days and rehearsed the story in later life.

narrative_blocks.header: His Cleveland diary documents the 1849 cholera epidemic
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel kept a clear daily diary from July 1849 through July 1850 in Cleveland, recording the cholera epidemic that he himself contracted and survived; an hour-by-hour account of President Zachary Taylor's death; steamboat-fire disasters; and his own self-recorded depression. The diary survives as an important primary source for early Cleveland history.

narrative_blocks.header: Migrated to California with childhood friend Mastick
narrative_blocks.body: Struck with 'California fever,' Samuel left Cleveland in 1852 with his childhood friend and law partner Edwin P. Mastick. He had been preceded by his brother Edwin Cowles' departure for Chicago — both sons of Dr. Edwin Weed Cowles fanning out from Austinburg Ohio in the same gold-rush decade.

sources.short: History of Ashtabula Co., Ohio (biographical sketch)

bio.photo_notes: Photograph shows Myra with her daughter Josephine Redding (HD3752, the WWI Croix-Rouge nurse who died age 26)

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of a Croix-Rouge nursing fatality
narrative_blocks.body: Myra's daughter Josephine Redding died in 1915 at age twenty-two while nursing in the French Red Cross during World War I. France awarded Josephine both the Médaille de Guerre and the Croix-Rouge diploma. The loss of an American niece in French wartime service marked one corner of the Cowles family's broader nineteenth-century expansion across Cleveland, Chicago, and California.

death.cause: died in sleep at home, 1000 Mason St, after seven-year illness

burial.plot_notes: Funeral Grace Cathedral, San Francisco, November 23, 1932

parents.father_name: Benjamin Barnard Redding (Sacramento mayor; California Secretary of State; Central Pacific Railroad land agent)
parents.mother_name: Mary Prescott Putnam Redding (1820 Boston - ?; Israel Putnam descendant)
parents.father_research_notes: Built as easter_egg X02774 in Stage 6V.
parents.mother_research_notes: Built as easter_egg X02779 in Stage 6X-10.

education.notes: Studied piano under Hugo L. Mansfeldt; gave first public concerts (piano duos with Mansfeldt) at age 11.
Entered 1871; graduated 1874. Began playing serious chess while a student here.
Entered 1877; graduated 1879. Directed Harvard's college orchestra; won the school's billiards tournament.

career.title: Attorney, Hall McAllister
career.organization: Hall McAllister legal firm, San Francisco
career.notes: Joined the firm at age 21 immediately after Harvard Law graduation.

career.title: Land-departments attorney
career.organization: Southern Pacific Transportation Co. + Central Pacific Railroad
career.notes: Argued Central Pacific Railroad v. Mudd (1881), CP v. Shackelford (1883), Southern Pacific Railroad Co. v. Dull (1884), SP v. Poole (1887), among other railroad-industry cases. Same organizations for which his father Benjamin had served as land agent.

career.title: Argued United States v. Kagama before the US Supreme Court
career.notes: At age 27 represented Kagama, a Yurok Native American accused of murder. Justice Samuel Freeman Miller's opinion UPHELD the constitutionality of the Major Crimes Act of 1885 — a foundational case in federal Indian-country jurisdiction still cited today.

career.title: Won Goldmark v. Kreling
career.organization: U.S. District Court, Northern District of California
career.notes: Early ruling protecting authors-composers rights; cited as legal precedent for sound-recording copyright in California prior to passage of the federal CLASSICS Act 1972.

career.title: Founder, Joseph D. Redding law firm
career.notes: Established his own firm with offices in both San Francisco and New York City.

career.title: Lead counsel, Hatzfeldt v. Huntington estate
career.notes: Represented Clara von Hatzfeldt in a contested-will case against the estate of Collis P. Huntington (railroad magnate). Won her a $6 million judgment; his $300,000 fee was at the time the highest amount of money earned by a lawyer in a court case involving only individual people (vs. corporations).

career.title: Pacific Coast Chess Champion
career.organization: Mercantile Library Association of San Francisco
career.notes: Beat Johannes Zukertort twice in 1884 (a blindfold simul, then individual; lost a third Evans-Gambit match); won the 1888 SF chess tournament; beat master George H. D. Gossip; defeated the entire Sacramento Chess Club by telegraph in 1889. Also played Wilhelm Steinitz, Emanuel Lasker, José Raúl Capablanca. 1907 guest speaker at the Manhattan Chess Club annual dinner.

career.title: President, San Francisco Arts Administration
career.notes: Held the presidency for several years.

career.title: Member, Bohemian Club
career.notes: Served as president. Wrote 'The Cremation of Care' (Druid Jinks, 1893) — Grove's signature opening ritual since 1893. Composed/wrote 'The Man of the Forest' (1902, first formal Grove play); 'The Atonement of Pan' (1912 music-drama with Henry Hadley). Also member of the Pacific-Union Club.

career.title: Co-founder, San Francisco Symphony
career.notes: One of the founding board members 1909 of the Musical Association — the organization that established the SF Symphony in 1911. Through his advocacy his friend composer Henry Kimball Hadley was appointed the orchestra's first music director; recommended Dutch violinist Eduard Tak as founding concertmaster.

career.title: Opera librettist
career.notes: Wrote libretto for Natoma (1911) — Victor Herbert music, Met Opera Philadelphia premiere with Mary Garden + John McCormack, Andreas Dippel company toured 30+ American cities. Also librettos for Hadley's The Atonement of Pan (1912) and Semper Virens (1923).

career.title: Composer, Fay Yen Fah
career.notes: His only opera as composer; premiered Monte Carlo Opera Feb 1925 — FIRST GRAND OPERA COMPOSED BY AN AMERICAN TO HAVE ITS PREMIERE IN EUROPE. France awarded him the Legion of Honour for the achievement. Later staged by the San Francisco Opera.

career.title: Songwriter and pianist
career.notes: Hits include 'Song to Hawaii' / 'Aloha to Hawaii' (1916, popular hit); 'Sweet Peggy O'Neil' (1920 by John McCormack); 'Hebe' (1900 piano piece by Victor); 'The Winds from Over the Sea' (1929 by Johnny Noble). 'Song to Hawaii' was recorded by multiple Hawaiian musicians.

career.title: Inducted, Société académique d'histoire internationale
career.notes: French scholarly society induction.

narrative_blocks.header: Argued landmark Indian-law case at twenty-seven
narrative_blocks.body: In 1886 at age twenty-seven, Joseph argued United States v. Kagama before the United States Supreme Court, representing Kagama, a Yurok Native American accused of murder. Justice Samuel Freeman Miller's opinion upheld the constitutionality of the 1885 Major Crimes Act — a foundational federal Indian-country ruling still cited today.

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote America's first opera produced in France
narrative_blocks.body: Joseph wrote the libretto for Natoma (1911), composed by Victor Herbert and starring Mary Garden, which the Andreas Dippel company toured to thirty-plus American cities. Fourteen years later, his own opera Fay Yen Fah premiered in 1925 at the Monte Carlo Opera — the first grand opera by an American to premiere in Europe.

narrative_blocks.header: France awarded him the Legion of Honour
narrative_blocks.body: France awarded Joseph the Legion of Honour for Fay Yen Fah, his 1925 grand opera. He was also inducted into the Société académique d'histoire internationale in 1919. His daughter Josephine, who died nursing in the French Croix-Rouge during World War I at twenty-six, also received the Légion d'Honneur.

narrative_blocks.header: Co-founded the San Francisco Symphony in 1911
narrative_blocks.body: Joseph was one of the founding board members of the 1909 Musical Association — the organization that established the San Francisco Symphony in 1911. Through his advocacy his friend Henry Kimball Hadley was appointed the orchestra's first music director, and Dutch violinist Eduard Tak was hired as founding concertmaster.

narrative_blocks.header: Pacific Coast chess champion in 1888
narrative_blocks.body: A talented amateur chess polymath, Joseph beat international master Johannes Zukertort twice in 1884, won the 1888 San Francisco tournament to become Pacific Coast champion, beat master George H. D. Gossip, and defeated the entire Sacramento Chess Club by telegraph in 1889.

narrative_blocks.header: Authored Bohemian Grove's 'Cremation of Care' ritual
narrative_blocks.body: President of the Bohemian Club and the San Francisco Arts Administration, Joseph wrote 'The Cremation of Care' — the elaborate funeral spectacle with Druid priests and chained captives that has opened the annual encampment at Bohemian Grove since 1893 — and 'The Man of the Forest,' the first formal Grove play, in 1902.

burial.plot_notes: Cypress Lawn Memorial Park, Colma CA. Inscription: 'Infirmière Auxiliaire, Union des Femmes de France, Croix Rouge, 1914-1915, Morte pour la France.'

notable.notable_blurb: WWI Croix-Rouge nurse; Légion d'Honneur, died young
notable.primary_url_label: Find a Grave: Josephine Redding (Cypress Lawn)

parents.father_name: Joseph Deighn Redding (Bohemian Club president; Natoma + Fay Yen Fah composer; Légion d'Honneur)

military_service.branch: Union des Femmes de France / French Red Cross
military_service.notes: Served with the Union des Femmes de France division of the Croix-Rouge française beginning 1914. Her tombstone bears the French inscription 'Infirmière Auxiliaire, Union des Femmes de France, Croix Rouge, 1914-1915, Morte pour la France' — the formal French phrase reserved for those who died in service to the nation.

cross_connections.display_label: WWII Iwo Jima Major cousin born after her death
WWII Red Cross Pacific cousin born after her death

narrative_blocks.header: WWI Croix-Rouge auxiliary nurse 'Morte pour la France'
narrative_blocks.body: Josephine served as an auxiliary nurse with the Union des Femmes de France division of the Croix-Rouge française beginning in 1914. Her gravestone at Cypress Lawn bears the French inscription 'Infirmière Auxiliaire, Union des Femmes de France, Croix Rouge, 1914-1915, Morte pour la France' — the formal phrase reserved for those who died in service to France.

narrative_blocks.header: Awarded the Légion d'Honneur for her war nursing
narrative_blocks.body: Josephine received the Légion d'Honneur for her work as a Croix-Rouge nurse during World War I, the same French national decoration her father Joseph would later receive in 1925 for composing Fay Yen Fah. She also earned the Croix-Rouge Médaille de Guerre.

narrative_blocks.header: Born France; died at twenty-six in New York
narrative_blocks.body: Born February 25, 1893 in France during her parents' European years, Josephine died in New York on October 7, 1919 at age twenty-six — only daughter of San Francisco lawyer-composer Joseph Deighn Redding and Myra Cowles Redding. She was buried with her Cowles grandparents at Cypress Lawn in Colma.

narrative_blocks.header: Helen died young
narrative_blocks.body: Helen died in 1868, aged about nine.

burial.plot_notes: Per Find a Grave Memorial 75869678 — buried as 'Anita Cowles Lavery'

narrative_blocks.header: Buried under Lavery — Cowles book misread it
narrative_blocks.body: She died 1944 in California, buried Cypress Lawn Memorial Park Colma with first husband Timothy Rearden and their daughter — gravestone reading 'Anita Cowles Lavery.' Her second husband's actual surname Lavery was rediscovered only after the Cowles family book had misread it as 'Janie Lacy.'

narrative_blocks.header: Married lawyer Timothy Rearden in San Francisco, 1888
narrative_blocks.body: Her first marriage in 1888 was to lawyer Timothy H Rearden of San Francisco — a Civil War veteran later a judge of the SF courts and member of the city's Chit-Chat Club literary circle. Their only child Anna Wooster Rearden was born December 1888 and went on to graduate UC Berkeley Class of 1912.

burial.plot_notes: Cypress Lawn, with wife Anita Cowles Lavery (HD3729) and daughter Anna/Nancy

notable.notable_blurb: San Francisco judge in the Bret Harte literary
notable.primary_url_label: Online Archive of California: Baeck Family Papers

parents.father_name: Denis Rearden (1800-1840) — d. when Timothy was about 1 year old
parents.mother_name: Nancy Rearden (1815-1896) — survived her son by 4 years
parents.father_research_notes: Per Find a Grave Memorial 78148625; not separately notable per project URL-anchor rule, not built as easter_egg.
parents.mother_research_notes: Per Find a Grave Memorial 78148628; not separately notable per project URL-anchor rule, not built as easter_egg.

career.title: Civil War service
career.notes: Brief Civil War service before relocating to California.

career.title: Clerk, U.S. Mint San Francisco
career.notes: Came to San Francisco 1863; worked at the Mint while studying law.

career.title: San Francisco lawyer
career.notes: Opened law practice in San Francisco around 1872.

career.title: San Francisco judge
career.notes: Served as a San Francisco judge until his 1892 death.

career.title: Author and literary scholar
career.notes: Member of the Chit-Chat Club; part of San Francisco's literary scene with Ambrose Bierce, Bret Harte, Fanny Osbourne, Ina Coolbrith, Porter Garnett. Serious student of languages and literature; book 'Petrarch and Other Essays' published posthumously.

narrative_blocks.header: San Francisco judge in the gilded age
narrative_blocks.body: Born Wooster Ohio 1839, Timothy graduated Kenyon College in 1859 and came to San Francisco in 1863 after brief Civil War service. He clerked at the U.S. Mint while studying law, opened practice around 1872, and served as a San Francisco judge until his death May 10, 1892.

narrative_blocks.header: Bret Harte and Ambrose Bierce literary circle
narrative_blocks.body: Timothy was a serious student of languages and literature, a member of the Chit-Chat Club, and part of San Francisco's golden-age literary scene including Ambrose Bierce, Bret Harte, Fanny Osbourne (later Stevenson), and Ina Coolbrith. His book 'Petrarch and Other Essays' was published after his death.

narrative_blocks.header: Married his friend Judge Cowles's daughter Anita
narrative_blocks.body: Timothy married Anita Cowles in 1888 — the daughter of his friend Hon. Samuel Cowles, San Francisco judge of the Court of Common Pleas. Their daughter Anna Rearden Baeck (1888-1970) became the first woman to write the 'Partheneia' pageant at UC Berkeley Class of 1912, and her papers along with Timothy's are preserved in the Baeck Family Papers at the Online Archive of California.

bio.extra_names: Also documented as 'Nancy Reardon Back/Baeck' on Find a Grave Memorial 75874428 and Wikitree (Reardon-1094) — likely informal family usage. OAC Baeck Family Papers (Bancroft Library) finding aid uses 'Anna Wooster Rearden Baeck' as the formal name; that archive is the project's primary source for this entry.

burial.plot_notes: Cypress Lawn Memorial Park, Colma — buried with husband John Erik Baeck and daughter Anne Charlotta. Per Find a Grave Memorial 75874428.

education.institution: Public schools of California and Illinois
education.notes: Per OAC Baeck Family Papers finding aid.

Class of 1912. Member of Prytanean Society (UC Berkeley women's honor society). Wrote the first 'Partheneia' to be given at the university by women students — performed in 1912, the year of her graduation.

career.title: Schoolteacher
career.notes: Taught school for several years after graduation, before her 1917 marriage. Per OAC finding aid.

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote UC Berkeley's first women-led Partheneia
narrative_blocks.body: A Class of 1912 graduate of UC Berkeley and member of the Prytanean Society women's honor society, Anna wrote the first Partheneia to be staged at the university by women students. It was performed her graduation year. The Partheneia became a Berkeley tradition that would run through the 1930s.

narrative_blocks.header: Berkeley resident from college through her death
narrative_blocks.body: After teaching school for several years, Anna married John Erik Baeck in 1917, settled in Berkeley, and remained there almost continuously until her death in 1970. Her papers and her father Judge Timothy Rearden's papers were donated to the Bancroft Library at UC Berkeley as the Baeck Family Papers (BANC MSS 74/101 c).

sources.short: OAC Baeck Family Papers finding aid (Bancroft Library, UC Berkeley)
Find a Grave Memorial 75874428 (under name 'Nancy Reardon Back')

narrative_blocks.header: Charlotte died young
narrative_blocks.body: Charlotte died in 1880, aged fifteen or sixteen.

burial.plot_notes: Fairlawn Section, Block 1, Graceland Cemetery — Badger family plot

career.title: President, A.S. Badger Lumber Company
career.notes: Owned and led A.S. Badger Lumber Company in Chicago.

burial.plot_notes: Badger family plot, Fairlawn Section, Graceland Cemetery
Badger family plot, Graceland Cemetery — GPS 41.9586913, -87.6602767

narrative_blocks.header: Wed an Oregon-born woman who lived to ninety-one
narrative_blocks.body: Edwin married Helen Jesse 'Holly' Pope, born 1892 in Portland Oregon, who survived him by twenty years and lived to ninety-one. Their two children both served in World War II — son Edwin Jr. as a US Army Air Corps Major who landed on Iwo Jima, daughter Mary Frances as a Red Cross worker in the Pacific Theater.

death.cause: died at Glen Oaks Nursing Home, Northbrook

burial.plot_notes: Fairlawn Section Block 1, Graceland Cemetery — GPS 41.9587253, -87.6602329 — buried with parents and siblings

notable.notable_blurb: WPA muralist; Chicago Society of Artists president

education.institution: School of the Art Institute of Chicago
education.notes: Began at age 6 at the Winnetka branch under Pauline Dohn Rudolph.

education.institution: School of the Art Institute of Chicago
education.notes: Studied with Matilda Vanderpoel and muralist John Warner Norton.

education.institution: School of the Art Institute of Chicago

career.title: Muralist
career.organization: Independent / Roycemore School / Fine Arts Building studio
career.notes: After 1925 European study trip with Matilda Vanderpoel, took studio at Chicago's Fine Arts Building and taught art at Roycemore School.

career.title: WPA Illinois Art Project muralist
career.organization: Works Progress Administration / Illinois Art Project
career.notes: Created murals for libraries, schools, hospitals, and field houses in Chicago, Oak Park, and Joliet IL after the Great Depression wiped out her family's fortune. Notable WPA murals: Joliet Township High School (1928, 1930); Audy Home (Chicago — Treasure Island themes, recently rediscovered); Cook County Children's Hospital; Hall of Science at the 1933 Century of Progress exhibition; Stevenson playground in Oak Park (Oak Park murals preserved by Oak Park Historical Society).

career.title: Exhibitor, Art Institute of Chicago
career.notes: 17 annual exhibitions at AIC between 1927 and 1943. Most celebrated work: Celery Pickers, No. 1 (1941 Chicago and Vicinity Exhibition); companion Celery Pickers, No. 2 (1943 International Watercolor Exhibition).

career.title: President, Chicago Society of Artists
career.notes: Held the presidency in 1942.

career.title: Studio mate at Tree Studios
career.notes: Shared working space with Rue Wintherbotham Shaw, president of the Arts Club of Chicago.

career.title: Art teacher
career.organization: School of the Art Institute of Chicago
career.notes: Returned to teach at SAIC in the 1940s alongside friends Ethel Spears and Kathleen Blackshear.

narrative_blocks.header: Chicago Society of Artists president in 1942
narrative_blocks.body: An indispensable presence in Chicago's interwar art community, Frances served as president of the Chicago Society of Artists in 1942. She was an active member of the Arts Club of Chicago and shared studio space at the Tree Studios in the mid 1930s with Rue Wintherbotham Shaw, the Arts Club's president.

narrative_blocks.header: WPA muralist after the family fortune collapsed
narrative_blocks.body: After the Great Depression wiped out the Badger family's fortune, Frances qualified for the WPA Illinois Art Project despite her socially prominent background. She painted murals for libraries, schools, hospitals, and field houses across Chicago, Oak Park, and Joliet — including the 1933 Century of Progress Hall of Science and a Treasure Island series at the Audy Home.

narrative_blocks.header: Seventeen exhibitions at the Art Institute of Chicago
narrative_blocks.body: Frances exhibited at the Art Institute of Chicago seventeen times between 1927 and 1943. Her most celebrated canvas, 'Celery Pickers No. 1' — a luminous oil depicting Black agricultural workers — appeared at the 1941 Chicago and Vicinity Exhibition. A gouache sequel followed in the 1943 International Watercolor Exhibition.

narrative_blocks.header: Studied and taught at the Art Institute herself
narrative_blocks.body: Frances began at the SAIC junior branch in Winnetka at age six and earned her studio art degree there in 1925 under Matilda Vanderpoel and John Warner Norton. She returned to teach in the 1940s alongside Ethel Spears and Kathleen Blackshear, and completed a formal B.F.A. there in 1949.

burialCemetery.name: Cypress Lawn Memorial Park
burialCemetery.notes: Founded 1892 Colma CA. Hooker-tree connections will be wired in Stage 6W-2: Hon. Samuel Cowles (HD3710), Anna Wooster Cowles (I00412), most of their seven children, Timothy H. Rearden (I00402), and Rearden-Baeck descendants.
